output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.95, "R1.3": 0.95, "R1.5": 0.4, "R1.6": 0.5}}

Summary: The section contains a fatal internal contradiction — it claims participants were blind to routing while simultaneously describing routing badges and an attribution survey that reveal the routing — which invalidates the headline methodological claim and the trust-attribution conclusion.

Critical contradiction (must be resolved before anything else)

Paragraph 1 states three blinding mechanisms: no model identifier in the response area, equalized latency via artificial delay, and a routing log that is never surfaced in the UI. Paragraph 2 then describes a "routing badge — a small green leaf for local responses, a blue cloud for cloud responses" that participants hover over before rating. A visible badge that distinguishes local from cloud is, by definition, a model identifier surfaced in the UI. The two paragraphs cannot both be true as written.

This is not a wording problem; it is a load-bearing collapse:

- The abstract-level contribution ("blind protocol… licenses our core claim that trust patterns can be attributed to response content alone") depends on participants not knowing backend identity.
- The headline finding ("2.3x longer on responses immediately after a cloud-badge hover") depends on participants knowing backend identity at hover time.
- The 71% attribution-survey accuracy figure only has an interpretation under blinding (it measures whether content alone leaks identity). If badges are visible, 71% is shockingly low and itself contradicts the badge mechanism; if badges were hidden during the survey but shown at rating time, this needs to be stated explicitly.

You need to pick one of three reconstructions and rewrite accordingly:

1. Truly blind: remove the badges entirely, drop the 2.3x hover finding, and keep only the attribution-survey result. The contribution becomes narrower.
2. Badged (not blind): drop the "blind protocol" framing and the "content alone" causal claim. Reframe as a study of how visible backend cues shape trust calibration. The hover-time finding becomes central; the attribution survey becomes a manipulation check.
3. Mixed-phase design: badges shown in some conditions/phases and not others. This must be described explicitly with a per-phase breakdown of which participants saw what when, and the analyses must be split accordingly.

Other substantive issues (assuming the contradiction is resolved)

- "Attributed to response content alone" is too strong even in the fully-blind version. Equalizing latency with an artificial delay on local responses does not control for response length, formatting differences, refusal patterns, hedging style, or the routing decision itself being correlated with prompt difficulty. The claim should be narrowed to "not attributable to explicit backend identity cues."
- The 2.3x figure lacks a denominator and a baseline. 2.3x longer than what — local-badge hovers, no-hover ratings, the participant's own median? Report the comparison group, the absolute durations, and a dispersion measure or test statistic.
- 71% attribution accuracy needs a chance baseline. With two classes the naive baseline is 50%, but the correct baseline is the marginal routing rate (if 80% of turns were routed local, always-guessing-local already achieves 80%). Report the routing distribution and a calibrated baseline.
- N=42 across two organizations with a within-subjects design — but the unit of analysis for 18,704 turns is unclear. Are the trust statistics computed per-turn, per-participant-then-averaged, or via a mixed-effects model with participant as a random effect? Per-turn pooling on this volume will produce spuriously tight intervals.
- "Mixed roles" and "two organizations" are mentioned but not characterized. For an HCI field deployment this is where reviewers will push hardest; at minimum give the role distribution and whether organization is a between-subjects factor.
- The difficulty estimator is named but never described or evaluated. If the routing decision is upstream of every reported finding, the paper needs at least a one-sentence characterization of how it routes and what fraction of turns went each way — otherwise the trust analysis is conditioned on an unspecified policy.
- "Tasks of their own choosing" plus a 30-minute-per-day floor leaves task type completely uncontrolled. Acknowledge this and report the observed task mix, or the external-validity claim is unsupported.
- The closing sentence promises that "the resulting calibration curves can be reused by designers." No calibration curves are presented in this section. Either move that promise to where the curves appear, or soften to "we report calibration data that…"

Prose-level

- "principal HCI contribution" and "central methodological commitment" in the same paragraph are both throat-clearing; pick one framing.
- "licenses our core claim" reads as overclaiming given the contradiction above; even after repair, prefer "supports" or "is intended to support."
- The hover-badge mechanism is introduced in paragraph 2 as an afterthought ("We additionally instrumented…") but turns out to be the source of the headline finding. It should be promoted to a first-class component of the method when paragraph 1 is reconciled.

Recommended order of operations: resolve the blinding contradiction first (this is a yes/no architectural decision about the study), then renegotiate the contribution claim to match, then address the statistics and the missing description of the difficulty estimator.

Uncertainty: I cannot tell from the text alone which of the three reconstructions reflects what the authors actually ran; the choice affects which findings survive. The statistical critiques assume the reported numbers come from per-turn pooling, which is the most common default but not stated.
